levels.name: Baby Steps
levels.description: Just like a toddler hearing sounds for the first time. We absorb, we don't memorize.

levels.name: First Words
levels.description: A toddler pointing at things and naming them. Simple, concrete, real.

levels.name: Little Sentences
levels.description: Stringing words together like a kid who just figured out they can ask for stuff.

levels.name: Street Talk
levels.description: Real conversations how real Russians actually talk. No textbook nonsense.

levels.name: Storyteller
levels.description: You can tell stories, argue, joke, and navigate life in Russian.

solar_systems.title: Greetings

sun.vibe: This is the universal 'hey!' — you'd say it to your friend, your neighbor, anyone you're chill with. It's warm, it's casual, it's the first thing every Russian kid learns to say.

pronunciation_guide.tip: Like 'free' but with a P at the start
Like 'vet' with a soft Y before the E

planets.vibe: This is the bro-nod of Russian greetings. Imagine two dudes bumping into each other — 'Yo, what's up?' That energy. Super casual, mostly guys use it.
The formal hello. You'd use it with your boss, a stranger, or someone older. It literally comes from the word for 'health' — so you're basically wishing someone good health just by saying hi.
Literally 'how are affairs/things?' but everyone uses it like 'how's it going?' It's so automatic that people sometimes answer before they even think about it.
The most Russian answer ever to 'how are you?' It means 'I'm alive, nothing's on fire, let's move on.' Russians don't do the American 'I'm great!' thing.

solar_systems.memorization_tip: pri-VYET sounds like "pre-VET" — imagine saying hi to your pet before a vet visit. You always greet them first! The word even has "vet" right in it, so picture walking into the vet's office and cheerfully saying hello.
solar_systems.culture_note: Russians don't smile at strangers — it's not rude, it's just how it works. A smile is saved for people you actually care about. So when a Russian smiles at you, it actually means something.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: Hey! How's it going?

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Normal. You how?' — notice the word order flip, Russian doesn't need 'are'

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Also normal' — тоже (TO-zhe) means 'also/too', super useful little word

grammar_nugget.title: Russian doesn't need 'am/is/are'
grammar_nugget.explanation: In English you say 'I am fine.' Russian just says 'I fine.' — Я нормально. There's no 'am.' The verb 'to be' in present tense? Russians threw it out. They decided it was unnecessary. This makes things simpler once you stop looking for it.

solar_systems.title: Yes & No

sun.vibe: Yes and no. The two most powerful words in any language. 'Да' is 'da' like your dad saying 'duh, obviously.' 'Нет' is a firm 'nyet' — and Russians aren't afraid to use it. No beating around the bush.

pronunciation_guide.tip: Like 'duh' but cleaner — mouth open, short and sharp
Like 'net' with a Y snuck in — NY-et. One syllable.

planets.vibe: The casual 'yeah/uh-huh.' Way more common than 'да' in real conversation. When your friend asks if you want tea and you go 'mhm' — that's ага energy.
Casual 'nah/nope.' The playful sibling of нет. Less serious, more chill. Like shaking your head with a little smile.
Means 'of course' — but the way it's spelled vs how it's said is totally different. Written: конечно. Spoken: канешна. The 'ч' (ch sound) turns silent. Russian loves doing this.

Means 'probably/maybe.' Russians use this ALL the time because they're not big on committing to definitive answers. 'Will you come?' — 'Наверное.' Translation: 'possibly, we'll see, don't hold me to it.'

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Да = think "DUH, obviously yes!" — it even sounds like agreement. Нет = think of a "NET" catching and stopping something — that's a no! The word literally sounds like it's blocking you.
solar_systems.culture_note: Russians are famously direct. If they don't want to do something, they'll say нет without sugar-coating it. This isn't rude in Russian culture — it's actually respectful because they're not wasting your time with fake politeness.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Want tea?' — хочешь (KHO-chesh) is 'you want' in casual form. No 'do you' needed.

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Yeah, let's do it.' — давай (da-VAY) is one of the most useful Russian words. It means 'let's go/come on/sure.'

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'With sugar?' — с (s) means 'with', сахар (SA-khar) is 'sugar', and the -ом ending means it's the 'with' form

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'No, without.' — без (byez) is 'without.' Super short, super efficient.

grammar_nugget.title: Cases: why word endings change
grammar_nugget.explanation: You just saw сахар become сахаром. Russian words change their endings depending on their role in the sentence. 'With sugar' needs the 'instrumental case' — the case for tools and companions. Don't panic about the name. Just notice: when you say 'with [something]', the something gets an -ом or -ой ending. You'll absorb the pattern naturally.

solar_systems.title: Pointing at Things

sun.vibe: This is THE pointing word. Imagine a toddler pointing at everything going 'THIS! THIS!' — that's это. It means 'this/that/it is.' One tiny word, does a ton of work. 'Это кот' = 'This is a cat.' 'Это мама' = 'This is mom.' Simple.

pronunciation_guide.tip: Like the 'e' in 'egg' — mouth slightly open
Like 'ta' in 'taco' — quick and light

planets.vibe: THE most important question for a learner. Point at stuff and ask. Что (shto) is 'what' — and yes, it's spelled with a ч but pronounced 'sh.' Russian spelling is trolling you again.
Same pattern but for people. Кто = who. 'Кто это?' while pointing at a photo is how every Russian kid learns family names.
Means 'here it is / there you go / voilà.' When someone hands you something — вот. When you finally find your keys — вот! It's the 'ta-da' word.
Here / there. Тут is 'right here, close to me.' Там is 'over there.' Short, punchy, used constantly. 'Он там' = 'He's over there.'

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Это (EH-ta) — think of pointing at something and saying "EH?? THAT!" The sound "eh" is what you naturally say when you see something and want to know what it is. You're already halfway to saying это!
solar_systems.culture_note: Russian doesn't have articles — no 'a' or 'the.' 'Это кот' is both 'this is a cat' and 'this is the cat.' Context tells you which. One less thing to worry about!

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'What this?' — pointing at something on the table

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'This borscht.' — борщ (borshch) is the famous beet soup

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'And this?' — а (a) means 'and/but', used to shift attention

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'This bread. Here, try.' — попробуй (pa-PRO-buy) means 'try it'

grammar_nugget.title: No articles, no problem
grammar_nugget.explanation: English has 'a' and 'the' and learners of English struggle with them for years. Russian said 'nah, we don't need those.' There are zero articles in Russian. Кот = a cat, the cat, just cat. Context handles the rest. This is one area where Russian is genuinely simpler than English.

solar_systems.title: I Want...

sun.vibe: I want. This is the toddler power phrase. Once a kid learns 'I want,' they run the household. Я (ya) is 'I', хочу (kha-CHOO) is 'want' in the I-form. Stick anything after it and you're communicating.

pronunciation_guide.tip: Like 'ya' in 'yard' — mouth wide open
Like clearing your throat gently — a soft 'kh' sound
Like 'choo choo' train — that exact sound

planets.vibe: Есть (yest) means 'to eat.' When you're hungry, this is survival Russian. Fun fact: есть ALSO means 'there is' in a different context.
Пить (peet) = to drink. Simple, essential. You'll use this one a lot.
Спать (spat) = to sleep. The universal human need, perfectly expressed in three syllables.
Different from wanting — it's about necessity. Literally 'to-me it-is-necessary.' Russians express need as something that happens TO you, not something you do.

common_words.meaning: I want to walk / go out
It's time for me to go

solar_systems.memorization_tip: хочу (kha-CHOO) — imagine a train going "CHOO CHOO!" That train is your desire engine — it WANTS to go! When you want something, your inner train says kha-CHOO! Also, the "kha" part sounds like a hungry person gasping before eating.
solar_systems.culture_note: Notice 'мне надо' — the 'I' isn't even the subject. Russian often frames experiences as things happening TO a person rather than things a person does. 'I'm cold' = 'Мне холодно' (to-me it-is-cold). It's a whole different way of seeing the world.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Eat you-want?' — notice the verb came first, totally natural in casual Russian

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'No, drink I-want' — flipped word order is fine, emphasis lands on 'drink'

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Water? Tea? What you-want?' — rapid-fire options, very natural
lines.natural: Water? Tea? What do you want?

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Let's-do tea.' — давай again, the Swiss Army knife word

grammar_nugget.title: Verb conjugation: it's not as scary as it looks
grammar_nugget.explanation: Хотеть (to want) changes based on who's wanting: я хочу (I want), ты хочешь (you want), он хочет (he wants). The ending shifts. Think of it like English: I want, he wantS — just Russian does it more. The pattern: -у/-ю for 'I', -ешь/-ишь for 'you (casual)', -ет/-ит for 'he/she.' You'll pick this up by hearing it.

solar_systems.title: I Like...

sun.vibe: I like (something). But here's the twist — in Russian, YOU don't like things. Things are pleasing TO YOU. 'Мне нравится кофе' literally = 'To-me is-pleasing coffee.' Coffee is doing the action, not you. Let it happen.

pronunciation_guide.tip: Start with M, slide right into 'nye' — like 'meh' but with an N
Quick NR combo then 'ah' — the R rolls slightly
Like 'vit' in 'vitamin'
Soft 'sa' at the end — almost whispered

planets.vibe: Just shove 'не' (nye) before the verb. Не = not. That's how you negate almost anything in Russian. Beautifully simple.
Музыка (MOO-zi-ka) = music. Notice it ends in -а, which tells you it's feminine. In Russian, even music has a gender.
Pure slang, used by everyone under 60. Literally means 'steep/sharp' but evolved into 'cool.'
Another 'cool' word. Comes from 'class' (as in classy). Круто and классно are practically interchangeable in casual talk.

common_words.meaning: I love it / I adore it

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Мне нравится — think of it backwards: things are "gravitating" toward you. You don't chase likes, likes come TO you. The "нрав" part sounds like "nerve" — things that please you hit a good nerve! Also: не нравится — just add "не" (neh) before anything to make it negative. One word flips everything.
solar_systems.culture_note: The backwards 'liking' structure shows up everywhere in Russian. 'I'm bored' = 'мне скучно' (to-me boring). 'I'm funny' — nope, 'мне смешно' (to-me laughable). Russian treats feelings as external forces acting on you.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'To-you is-pleasing this song?' — тебе is the 'to you' form of ты

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Yeah, cool! And to-you?' — bouncing the question back

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'To-me not really.' — не очень is the polite way to say 'meh'

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Well okay, to-each their-own.' — a common Russian saying
lines.natural: Fair enough, to each their own.

grammar_nugget.title: Gender: everything has it
grammar_nugget.explanation: Every Russian noun is masculine, feminine, or neuter. Usually you can tell by the ending: consonant = masculine (стол, table), -а/-я = feminine (музыка, music), -о/-е = neuter (окно, window). This matters because adjectives and past tense verbs match the gender. Don't memorize rules — just start noticing the patterns.

solar_systems.title: Where?

sun.vibe: Where? — Another survival word. 'Где туалет?' (Where's the bathroom?) will save your life. One syllable, maximum utility.

pronunciation_guide.tip: G and D blend together, then 'yeh' — say it fast like one punch: GDYEH

planets.vibe: Two words for the same thing — здесь is slightly more proper, тут is more casual. In everyday talk, тут wins.
Там covers everything from 'across the room' to 'in another city.' Context is king.
'Это далеко?' = 'Is it far?' — essential question when someone gives you directions and you're about to walk 3 kilometers in the snow.
Navigation basics. На- is a prefix meaning 'to/toward.' Право = right side, лево = left side. Прямо = directly/straight.

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Где (gdyeh) — it sounds like "G, deh?" as if you're lost and grunting "where?!" Also think: Gotta know Directions Everywhere. For directions: на-ПРАВО (right) has "прав" which means "correct" — the right way is the correct way!
solar_systems.culture_note: If you ask for directions in Russia, be prepared for the answer to include landmarks, not street names. 'Go past the big grey building, turn at the kiosk, it's behind the playground.' Russians navigate by vibes, not GPS.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Sorry, where metro?' — извини (iz-vi-NEE) is casual 'excuse me/sorry'

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Over there, straight and to-left.' — вон (von) = 'over there' with a pointing gesture implied
lines.natural: Over there, go straight and turn left.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Far?' — one word, gets the job done

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'No, minutes five.' — number comes AFTER the noun in casual speech sometimes

grammar_nugget.title: Dropping pronouns
grammar_nugget.explanation: Notice 'Далеко?' — no 'it', no 'is.' Russian lets you drop pronouns when context makes things obvious. You don't need 'это далеко?' when you're clearly talking about the metro. Less words, more efficiency. Russian respects your intelligence.

solar_systems.title: Opinions

sun.vibe: I think that... — Now you're expressing opinions! Думать (DOO-mat) = to think. Что (shto) = that/what. This structure lets you say almost any opinion: 'Я думаю, что это круто' = 'I think that's cool.'

pronunciation_guide.tip: Quick 'yah' — you already know this one
Like 'do' in English
Like 'ma' as in mama
Like 'you' — so together: doo-mah-yoo
NOT 'chto' — the ч is pronounced as 'sh' here

planets.vibe: Quicker than 'я думаю что.' Slap it at the start of any sentence to make it an opinion.
Literally 'can be.' Russians shorten it to 'может' in fast speech. The ultimate non-committal phrase.
You'll hear this A LOT. Russians use it as a filler, a deflection, and sometimes genuinely. Accompanied by a shrug, it's a complete sentence.
Another backwards construction — the seeming happens TO you. More tentative than 'я думаю.' Good for when you're not sure but want to share a thought.

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Я думаю — "DOO-ma-yoo" sounds like "DO MY YOU" — like asking yourself "do I think...?" Also: что is always pronounced "shto" not "chto." Think: SHocking That nObody pronounces it how it's spelled!
solar_systems.culture_note: Russians value directness but also hedge a lot with 'может быть,' 'наверное,' and 'мне кажется.' It's not indecisiveness — it's intellectual honesty. They won't state something as fact unless they're sure.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'How to-you this film?' — как тебе = what do you think of
lines.natural: What'd you think of the movie?

lines.speaker: B
lines.russian: По-моему, нормально. Не шедевр, но ок.
lines.breakdown: 'In-my-opinion, normal. Not masterpiece, but ok.'
lines.natural: It was alright. Not a masterpiece, but okay.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'But to-me not pleased.' — past tense of нравиться

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Seriously? Why?'

grammar_nugget.title: Past tense is shockingly easy
grammar_nugget.explanation: Take any verb, remove -ть from the end, add -л (masculine), -ла (feminine), -ло (neuter), or -ли (plural). That's it. Думать → думал/думала/думали. Хотеть → хотел/хотела/хотели. No 'did,' no 'was doing,' no 'had been doing.' One past tense covers everything.

solar_systems.title: Permission & Politeness

sun.vibe: Can I...? / Is it okay to...? — The magic permission word. 'Можно воды?' = 'Can I have some water?' Можно opens doors — literally and figuratively. It's polite without being stiff.

pronunciation_guide.tip: Like 'mosh' (mosh pit) but with a ZH sound — tongue buzzing
Quick 'nah' sound — together: MOZH-nah

planets.vibe: The opposite of можно. If someone says нельзя, the door is closed. 'Тут нельзя курить' = 'You can't smoke here.'
Давайте is the polite/plural version. 'Давай пойдём' = 'Let's go.' 'Давай!' alone = 'Let's do it!'
Does double duty. In fast speech it gets crunched to 'пжалста.' Also used when handing someone something.
Comes from 'спаси бог' (God save you) — compressed over centuries. In texts, Russians abbreviate it to 'спс.'

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Можно (MOZH-nah) — think "MOSH pit? NAH, can I?" You're asking permission before moshing. For спасибо — it comes from "спаси бог" (God save you). Think: when someone helps you, you're saying "you SAVED me!" That gratitude is built into the word.
solar_systems.culture_note: Можно/нельзя is how Russians frame most permissions. Instead of 'may I?' — it's impersonal. 'Is it possible?' vs 'Is it not possible?' This removes the personal element, making it feel less confrontational.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Possible here to-sit?' — сесть (syest) = to sit down

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Yes, of course, sit-down.' — садись (sa-DEES) is the casual command

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: Thanks!

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'You're welcome. Coffee you-want?'

grammar_nugget.title: Impersonal sentences
grammar_nugget.explanation: Можно, нельзя, надо, холодно, жарко — these are 'impersonal' constructions. There's no subject. It's not 'I can' or 'you must' — it's just 'possible' or 'necessary' floating in space, and context tells you who it applies to. It's like the universe is making the statement, not a person.

solar_systems.title: Exclamations

sun.vibe: The PG-rated Russian exclamation. Literally means 'pancake' but it's used like 'dang!/shoot!/crap!' It's the polite substitute for a much stronger word. Everyone uses блин — kids, grandmas, professionals mid-meeting.

pronunciation_guide.tip: Like 'clean' but with a B — one syllable, quick and punchy

planets.vibe: Literally 'fir trees and sticks.' Adorably old-fashioned but still used. The family-friendly exclamation.
'Это фигня' = 'That's nonsense/garbage.' Mildly rude but socially acceptable. Used to dismiss anything unimpressive.
Literally 'nothing to oneself' which makes zero sense literally, but it's pure surprise/amazement. Something shocking happens? Ничего себе!
'Ладно' can mean 'okay fine' (agreeing), 'okay fine!' (annoyed agreement), or 'alright then' (wrapping up). Tone is everything.

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Блин literally means "pancake" (and it IS the word for Russian crepes/blini). So imagine burning your pancakes and yelling "BLEEN!" — it perfectly captures that mild frustration. Next time you make pancakes, you'll remember this word forever.
solar_systems.culture_note: Russian has an incredibly rich world of casual expressions and mild swearing. Блин sits at the top of the acceptable pyramid. Below it are words that every Russian knows but textbooks pretend don't exist. For now, блин will cover 90% of your frustration-expression needs.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Dang, I was-late!' — опоздал (a-paz-DAL) = was late (masculine past)

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'No way, by how-much?' — на сколько = by how much (time)

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'By half-hour. Boss will-kill.' — полчаса = half hour
lines.natural: Half an hour. My boss is gonna kill me.

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Okay, say that traffic-jams were.' — пробки = traffic jams

grammar_nugget.title: Perfective vs Imperfective (the big one)
grammar_nugget.explanation: Russian has two versions of most verbs. Опаздывать (imperfective) = to be in the process of being late. Опоздать (perfective) = to have arrived late, done deal. Think of it as 'doing' vs 'done.' Я опаздываю = 'I'm running late (right now).' Я опоздал = 'I was late (it happened).' This is THE thing that makes Russian verbs tricky. Don't try to master it — just start noticing the pairs.

solar_systems.title: Filler Words

sun.vibe: Basically / long story short / so basically. THE filler word of modern Russian. It means 'shorter' literally, and people use it when they're about to summarize or just... ramble. 'Короче, я пришёл, а там никого.'

pronunciation_guide.tip: Short 'kah' — like the start of 'car'
Rolled R then 'oh'
Like 'che' in Che Guevara — together: kah-RO-cheh

planets.vibe: The Russian equivalent of saying 'like' every other word. 'Он типа обиделся' = 'He like, got offended.' Casual speech only.
When someone says something confusing, hit them with 'в смысле?' and watch them try to explain.
Used to introduce surprising news or gossip. 'Прикинь, он уволился!' = 'Get this, he quit!'
Borrowed feel from English but fully Russian. Can be a question or emphasis: 'Реально круто!' = 'Genuinely cool!'

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Короче literally means "shorter." Think of someone starting a long rambling story and you say "make it SHORTER!" — that's exactly how Russians use it: "long story short..." (then they proceed to tell the long version anyway). Типа = just think of the English word "type" — "it's типа (type of) like that."
solar_systems.culture_note: Короче and типа are the verbal tics of modern Russian, especially among younger people. Using them appropriately will make you sound way more natural than perfect textbook Russian ever could.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Imagine, what yesterday was!' — вчера (vche-RA) = yesterday
lines.natural: Dude, you won't believe what happened yesterday!

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'What?'

lines.speaker: A
lines.russian: Короче, иду я по улице, и типа встречаю Машу.
lines.breakdown: 'Basically, walk I along street, and like run-into Masha.'
lines.natural: So basically, I'm walking down the street and I run into Masha.

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Really? And what?' — и что is 'and what happened next?'

grammar_nugget.title: Word order is flexible (but not random)
grammar_nugget.explanation: 'Иду я по улице' — the verb came first, then the subject. In English this would be weird. In Russian, word order is flexible because case endings tell you who's doing what. The word that comes first gets emphasis. 'Я иду' = neutral. 'Иду я' = emphasizes the walking. Play with word order to control what feels important.

solar_systems.title: Telling Stories

sun.vibe: When I was little... — Now you're telling stories about the past. Когда = when, был = was (masculine), маленький = little/small. Change был to была if you're female. This opener works for any childhood story.

pronunciation_guide.tip: Like 'cog' in a machine
Like 'да' (yes) — together: kahg-DAH
Like 'bill' — short and flat
Like 'ma'
Soft L then 'yen'
Like 'key' — together: MAH-lyen-kiy

planets.vibe: 'Раньше тут был парк' = 'There used to be a park here.' Nostalgia in one word.
Your story-connecting word. 'Сначала... потом...' = 'First... then...' Every story needs a потом.
The plot-twist word. 'И вдруг — звонок!' = 'And suddenly — a phone call!' Drama in two syllables.

planets.meaning: In the end / as a result
planets.vibe: How you wrap up a story. 'В итоге мы опоздали' = 'In the end we were late.'

common_words.meaning: Once upon a time / one day

solar_systems.memorization_tip: Story words follow a natural timeline — learn them in order: Однажды (once upon a time) → Сначала (at first) → Потом (then) → Вдруг (suddenly!) → В итоге (in the end). Practice by telling any simple story using just these five connector words. Even in English first: "Once, at first... then... suddenly... in the end."
solar_systems.culture_note: Russians are natural storytellers. A simple 'how was your day?' can turn into a 10-minute narrative with characters, plot twists, and a moral conclusion. Learning these narrative connectors is essential for both telling and following Russian stories.

lines.speaker: A
lines.breakdown: 'Basically, listen what was.' — слушай (SLOO-shay) = listen

lines.speaker: B
lines.breakdown: 'Go ahead, tell.' — рассказывай = tell/narrate

lines.speaker: A
lines.russian: Сначала всё было нормально. Потом вдруг позвонил Дима и говорит...
lines.breakdown: 'At first all was normal. Then suddenly called Dima and says...' — notice present tense mid-story, totally natural
lines.natural: At first everything was fine. Then out of nowhere Dima calls and goes...

lines.speaker: B
lines.russian: Ой, не могу, и что он сказал?
lines.breakdown: 'Oh, I can't (take it), and what he said?' — не могу = the suspense!
lines.natural: Oh my god, what did he say?

grammar_nugget.title: Historic present tense in storytelling
grammar_nugget.explanation: Notice 'позвонил Дима и говорит' — 'called Dima and SAYS.' He switches from past to present tense mid-sentence. This is totally normal in casual Russian storytelling. It makes the story feel alive. English does this too ('So I'm at the store and this guy WALKS up to me...') but Russian does it even more freely.
